coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence sensor utilizing label-free nitrogen self-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was developed for the sensitive, selective, and rapid determination of dichloran fungicide, popularly used in agricultural and horticultural fields. The NCDs were prepared from maleic anhydride and diethylenetriamine via a one-step pyrolysis process. They demonstrated strong blue fluorescence emission with a quantum yield of 12 %. With the addition of dichloran, the fluorescence emission of NCDs was quenched, attributed to the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. They demonstrated outstanding sensitivity to dichloran with a linear range between 1.0 and 50.0 µM and a remarkably low detection limit of 7.6 nM, the best reported date. The sensing process could be repeatedly and rapidly generated within 30 s. Additionally, the NCDs exhibited selectivity towards dichloran amidst interferences, including common metal ions, organic chemicals, and other fungicides. The detection of dichloran in carrots, grapes, and drinking water was successfully accomplished using NCDs, yielding satisfactory recovery results ranging between 95.1 and 108.7 %. Moreover, a paper-based sensor based on NCDs as sensing probes was demonstrated to observe the fluorescence quenching towards dichloran, with a detection limit of 4.24 µM. It also showed high efficacy in distinguishing and selectively detecting dichloran against interferences. Therefore, this work contributes to the development of efficient and portable detection platforms with applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural fields.

(a) Excitation-dependence fluorescence emission spectra and (b) UV–vis absorbance spectrum and the maximum fluorescence emission spectrum of NCDs; inset shows the NCD solutions under room light (left) and 365-nm UV light (right).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs with (blue line) and without (black line) the addition of 100 µM dichloran; inset shows the NCDs solution (left) in the presence of 100 µM dichloran (right) under 365-nm UV light. (b) UV–vis spectrum of dichloran (black line) and the fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission spectra (blue line) of the NCDs. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs mixed with various concentrations of dichloran (from 1 to 200 µM) at pH 7. (b) The plot of F0/F versus concentrations of dichloran; inset figure shows the linear range of the plot. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs mixed with 100 µM various interferences and 100 µM dichloran (control sample is the NCDs containing 100 µM dichloran). (d) The relative fluorescence intensities in percentage of NCDs in the presence of interference with dichloran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence sensor utilizing label-free nitrogen self-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was developed for the sensitive, selective, and rapid determination of dichloran fungicide, popularly used in agricultural and horticultural fields. The NCDs were prepared from maleic anhydride and diethylenetriamine via a one-step pyrolysis process. They demonstrated strong blue fluorescence emission with a quantum yield of 12 %. With the addition of dichloran, the fluorescence emission of NCDs was quenched, attributed to the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. They demonstrated outstanding sensitivity to dichloran with a linear range between 1.0 and 50.0 µM and a remarkably low detection limit of 7.6 nM, the best reported date. The sensing process could be repeatedly and rapidly generated within 30 s. Additionally, the NCDs exhibited selectivity towards dichloran amidst interferences, including common metal ions, organic chemicals, and other fungicides. The detection of dichloran in carrots, grapes, and drinking water was successfully accomplished using NCDs, yielding satisfactory recovery results ranging between 95.1 and 108.7 %. Moreover, a paper-based sensor based on NCDs as sensing probes was demonstrated to observe the fluorescence quenching towards dichloran, with a detection limit of 4.24 µM. It also showed high efficacy in distinguishing and selectively detecting dichloran against interferences. Therefore, this work contributes to the development of efficient and portable detection platforms with applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fungicides are crucial for maintaining global food security, despite their risks. They are frequently used to protect agricultural crops and plants from fungi, which can lead to disease, reduced yields, and low product quality [1]. Dichloran (2,6-dichloro-4-nitroaniline) is a fungicide applied to a variety of fruits and vegetables, such as carrots, grapes, and strawberries, for both pre- and post-harvest diseases [2]. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in the United States disclosed the presence of dichloran residues in several agricultural crops. Moreover, dichloran is used annually, exceeding 200,000 lb in the United States [3]. However, a high dose of dichloran in human body could pose severe health risks, including damage to organs, respiratory paralysis, or even death [4]. Hence, it is crucial to detect dichloran with a rapid, selective, and sensitive method to protect our health.

Fungicide detection can be performed using several available techniques, including gas chromatography [5], high-performance liquid chromatography [6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [7], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8]. Despite their established reliability, these methods have limitations, such as complexity, high costs, and challenges in real-time and on-site detection processing. In contrast, the use of fluorescence-based sensors has gained attention because of its cost-effectiveness, simplicity, ultrasensitivity, portability, and fast processing [9,10]. Central to this method are the nanomaterials serving as fluorescent sensing probes, which must be particularly selective and sensitive to analytes [11].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbon-based nanomaterials consisting of a carbon core with diverse functional groups on the surface. Because of their outstanding fluorescence, biocompatibility, high water solubility, and ease of preparation, CDs have been applied in sensing [12–17]. Moreover, heteroatom doping has been widely used in CD preparation. The doping of the heteroatoms provides the advantage of introducing functional group diversity to the resulting CDs. This diversity creates binding sites for other molecules, ions, or analytes, making the doped CDs favorable as sensing probes [18]. Zhao et al. demonstrated a hydrothermal treatment of chitosan chains at 180 °C, yielding nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs). They exhibited outstanding sensitivity in detecting Fe3+, with a low detection limit of 0.15 µM [19]. Zhang et al. synthesized NCDs using citric acid and urea as precursors with a high quantum yield of 25.4 % via hydrothermal method. The fluorescent NCDs detected curcumin via the inner filter effect with a detection limit of 0.08 µM [20]. Furthermore, the interaction between heteroatom-doped CDs and analytes can give rise to different mechanisms, including inner filter effect (IFE) [21], photo-induced electrode transfer (PET) [22], and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [23], ultimately altering the optical properties of CDs. Therefore, heteroatom-doped CDs have been used as fungicide sensors. For example, Yang et al. utilized nitrogen and phosphorus-doped CDs (N- and P-doped CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to determine carbendazim. The green fluorescence emission of N- and P-doped CDs quenched by AuNPs through FRET was selectively reduced by adding carbendazim, resulting in a low detection limit of 0.002 µM [23]. Zou et al. synthesized CDs doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N- and S-doped CDs) from L-cysteine and then applied them as a fluorescence fluazinam sensor. A strong IFE occurred during the fluorescence quenching of N, S-doped CDs in the existence of fluazinam. The fabricated sensor exhibited good sensitivity, with a detection limit of 10 nM in a linear range from 0.05 to 4 µM [24]. Based on the previous works, heteroatom-doped CDs are capable of being used as sensing probes for fungicide sensors via several mechanisms.

A sensor for detecting dichloran fungicide has never been previously developed from CDs. In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor based on label-free NCDs for the sensitive, selective, and rapid determination of dichloran was developed (Fig. 1 ). The self-passivated fluorescent NCDs were prepared from maleic anhydride (MA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as carbon and nitrogen sources via a one-step pyrolysis process. As fluorescent sensing probes, NCDs can detect dichloran based on the fluorescence method. Furthermore, various effects, including pH, time, and dichloran concentrations, on the NCD’s fluorescence emission were investigated. The reliability and practicality of detecting dichloran using NCDs were evaluated in carrots, grapes, and drinking water samples. A paper-based sensor could be applied to dichloran determination amidst interferences. This work also contributes to developing efficient and portable detection platforms with applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural fields.

All reagents were at least of analytical reagent grade with purity higher than 99 % and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted. Maleic anhydride (99 %) and diethylenetriamine (98.5 %) were bought from Acros Organics. Acetonitrile was obtained from Carlo Erba (HPLC grade, 99.9 %). Nitric acid (96 % w/w) was acquired from Carlo Erba. Carrots, grapes, and drinking water were obtained from a local market. A dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1,000 Da was purchased from Membrane Filtration Products, Inc. Dichloran stock solution was prepared in ethanol, while others were prepared in deionized (DI) water with a resistivity exceeding 18 MΩ·cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs from MA and DETA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent NCDs were synthesized by first dissolving MA (4.0 g) and DETA (2.0 g) in DI water (15 mL) to obtain the transparent solution. The clear solution was introduced into a crucible containing nitric acid solution (2.5 M, 10 mL) and subjected to pyrolysis at 250 °C for 4 h in air using a furnace. The mixture solution was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. It was diluted with DI water to a total volume of 75 mL and then ultrasonicated for 15 min. Next, the mixture was filtered to separate the yellow solution, which was then neutralized to pH 7 before undergoing dialysis for 72 h. The supernatant was gathered following the centrifugation of the yellow solution at 7,000 rpm for 10 min. Lastly, the obtained supernatant was freeze-dried to yield a light-yellow solid of NCDs (3.21 mg, 0.05 %).

A transmission electron microscope from FEI Corporation (TEM; TecnaiTM G2 F20) was utilized to examine the size and morphology of the NCDs. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos Analytical, Manchester, UK) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Spectrum 2000) were used to evaluate the structural characterization. A HORIBA Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer was employed to measure optical properties.

A quantum yield of the NCDs was calculated using the slope method based on previous works [25,26]. The absorbance and fluorescence peak areas of various concentrations of NCDs (0.025, 0.04, 0.05, 0.075, and 0.1 mg mL−1) and quinine sulfate (0.0075, 0.01, 0.015, 0.02, and 0.04 mg mL−1) in sulfuric acid solution (0.1 M) as a reference (quantum yield = 54 %, excitation at 349 nm) were measured.

The quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescent NCD solution was evaluated while dispersed in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, using the following equation (1) [27],
(1)

Φ represents the quantum yield, where the subscripts C and R correspond to the NCD and the reference material, respectively, with quinine sulfate as a reference (ΦR = 54 %). Additionally, I denotes the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and n is the refractive index.

The NCD solution in DI water (0.5 mg mL−1, 3 mL) was added to the dichloran solution in ethanol (200 μM, 3 mL). For quantitative analysis, different volumes of dichloran stock solution (500 μM) were successively mixed thoroughly with the NCD solution (0.5 mg mL−1, 3 mL). Subsequently, HCl and NaOH were utilized to adjust the pH. Fluorescence measurements were conducted at an optimal excitation wavelength of 320 nm, with the maximum emission wavelength recorded at 400 nm. The fluorescence intensity was determined by comparing the NCD solution containing dichloran analyte (F) with the fluorescence intensity of the pure NCD solution (F0 ). A set of F0 /F and F/F0 values was used on the y-axis of the plots.

Carrot, grape, and drinking water samples were selected for the real sample analysis. Small pieces of carrot and grape pericarps (20 g) were separately immersed in ethanol (60 mL) and subjected to ultrasonication for 60 min. The resulting solution was then filtered. The ethanol (60 mL) was mixed with a water sample (20 mL). All samples were kept at 4 °C before use. Standard dichloran solutions (0.0–100.0 µM), carrot, grape, and drinking water samples spiked with the standard dichloran solutions (0.0–50.0 µM) were prepared. All samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon filter. The dichloran concentrations were determined using fluorescence and HPLC techniques. The HPLC measurement with a 350 nm UV detector, a C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d., 5 µm particle diameter), and a guard column (12.5 mm × 4.6 mm i.d., 5 µm particle diameter) was performed (LC-20AT, series Shimadzu). The mobile phase contained an acetonitrile/water (1:1 v/v) mixture. The standard and sample solutions were injected into a 20-µL feeding loop at 25 °C with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. Furthermore, the validity of the findings was examined using the t-test and F-test.

The selectivity of the NCDs towards dichloran was evaluated using the common metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl-,
N O

), chemicals (ascorbic acid and citric acid), and other fungicides (tebuconazole and vinclozolin) as the interference substances. The fluorescence intensities of the NCDs mixed with dichloran (100 µM) were measured with the addition of interferences (100 µM), while the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs/dichloran was assessed as the control sample under optimized conditions.

A paper-based sensor serves as a portable and on-site detection platform. In this work, a paper-based sensor was prepared by immersing the cellulose-based Whatman filter paper (1 × 1 cm2) in the NCD solution (0.5 mg mL−1), followed by drying at 50 °C for 30 min. Various concentrations of dichloran (1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 75.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 400.0, and 500.0 µM, 0.1 mL each) were applied to the NCD-coated filter papers (NCD papers) and the NCD papers were then dried at 50 °C for 15 min. Additionally, the selectivity of the NCDs towards dichloran was investigated by introducing interference substances (100.0 µM, 0.1 mL each) onto the NCD-paper under the same conditions described above. The fluorescence emission of NCD paper was considered the control sample. The variations in fluorescence emission were documented with a digital phone camera (iPhoneXS) under 365 nm UV light, and the R, G, and B values were obtained based on the color model in which red, green, and blue are the primary colors that are added to produce the broad array of colors. The R, G, and B values from the images were assessed using Photoshop (Adobe®). The sensitivity of the NCD-paper was determined by equation (2): Five spots were taken for the calculation of the average values.
(2)
Sumofred - g r e e n - b l u e ( R G B ) v a l u e s = R e d ( R ) + G r e e n ( G ) + B l u e ( B )
The sum of RGB values was used to produce a plot against the concentration of dichloran, which was then used to obtain the linear range and LOD for the paper-based sensor.

In this work, the self-passivated NCDs were synthesized from MA and DETA. MA as a carbon source was commonly used in the synthesis of CDs [28,29]. Moreover, both MA and DETA contain carboxyl and amino groups, offering the advantage of introducing diverse functional groups to the resulting CDs [14]. This diversity establishes various binding sites for analytes. Additionally, DETA can facilitate nitrogen doping in carbon dots due to its richness in nitrogen atoms. Wang et al. reported synthesized CDs using DETA as a passivating agent and citric acid as a carbon precursor with a high quantum yield of 68 % [30]. They implied that enhancing nitrogen contents and conjugated π-domains provided a superior photoluminescence efficiency for the CDs. Hence, MA and DETA are suitable precursors for NCD preparation. They were successfully carbonized in the presence of nitric acid via a one-step pyrolysis at 250 °C for 4 h. Using nitric acid as an oxidizing agent can help break down the carbon precursor into smaller fragments. It can also introduce nitrogen and oxygen functionalities into the CDs, increasing the photoluminescence effectiveness of the CDs [31].

The size and morphology of the NCDs were examined using TEM. The TEM image shows that the NCDs are quasi-spherical in shape, monodispersed, and homogeneous (Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was observed from a high-resolution TEM image, consistent with prior works (inset of Fig. 2a). As displayed in Fig. 2b, a histogram of the NCD diameter exhibited the nanoparticle size distribution with an average size of 8.5 nm.

FT-IR and XPS techniques were utilized to further characterize the chemical bond structures and the chemical states of the NCDs. A characteristic broad vibration band at 3400 cm−1 was observed, corresponding to the hydroxyl (O–H) and amine (N–H) groups. The vibration peaks at 2924, 1578–1692, and 1073 cm−1 corresponded to alkyl (C–H), alkenyl/carbonyl (CC/CO), and C-O stretching, respectively [10,32,33]. Moreover, the existence of vibration peak at 1383 cm−1 was due to the C–H bending (Fig. 3 a) [34]. The results indicated the presence of abundant hydroxyl, alkenyl, carbonyl, and amino groups on the NCDs. Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum clearly demonstrated that the NCDs contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements, which can be observed at 285.1, 399.1, and 532.1 eV with atomic concentrations of 49.29, 7.18, and 31.34 %, respectively (Fig. 3b) [35]. The results confirmed that the NCDs derived from MA and DETA were self-doped with nitrogen. The C1s spectrum exhibited three different bonding states of carbon at 284.9, 286.5, and 288.3 eV, which belonged to C–C, C–OH, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3c) [36]. The O1s spectrum presented four peaks of C–OH, C–O–C (aliphatic), CO (aromatic), and CO at 531.5, 532.7, 533.9, and 535.6 eV (Fig. 3d) [37,38]. Additionally, the high-resolution spectrum of N1s showed four peaks at 399.2, 399.9, 401.2, and 407.1 eV, corresponding to C–NC, C–N–C, C–NH2, and −O-NO2, respectively (Fig. 3e) [36,39,40]. The O–NO2 groups were caused by nitric acid utilized in the NCD synthesis. The NCDs exhibited highly dominant carbon, oxygen, and especially nitrogen, according to the FT-IR and XPS results. The elevated nitrogen content strongly indicates the successful nitrogen doping of the CDs. Notably, the pronounced abundance of heteroatoms can enhance the photoluminescence observed on the NCDs, making them well-suited for applications as fluorescent sensing probes.

The fluorescence emission properties of the NCDs were evaluated based on various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4 a). An increase in the excitation wavelength from 320 to 460 nm resulted in a redshift of the emission wavelength. This indicated an excitation-dependent behavior caused by various surface states and emissive states from abundant self-passivated nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the NCD surface [41]. The NCDs exhibited the maximum emission wavelength located at 400 nm, with a high quantum yield of 12 %, under a 320 nm-excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b). The NCD solution appeared as a clear yellow liquid under room light, while a bright blue color emission was observed under 365-nm UV light (inset of Fig. 4b). Moreover, the absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectrum located at 285 nm indicate the π–π* transition of the CC bonds in the NCD core, while the shouldering peak at 325 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bonds (Fig. 4b) [42]. The results suggested that the NCDs consisted of a large aromatic core with various emissive sites on the NCD surface [43].

The fluorescent NCDs were employed for a dichloran fungicide sensor. The fluorescence emission properties of the NCDs with dichloran were then compared with those of the pure NCDs. It was found that the presence of dichloran led to a notable decrease in the fluorescence emission of the NCDs, suggesting a non-radiative interaction between dichloran and NCDs, as shown in Fig. 5 a. In addition, the presence of dichloran clearly caused the NCD solution’s color to change from bright blue to dark blue under UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 5a). To investigate the detection mechanism, the UV–vis absorption of dichloran and the fluorescence excitation and emission of NCDs were investigated (Fig. 5b). It was found that the absorption band of dichloran overlapped with the NCD’s excitation and emission spectra located at 325 and 400 nm, respectively. Thus, it is possible to effectively quench the fluorescence signal from NCDs through two processes: 1) the competitive energy absorption between donor and acceptor and 2) the emission energy transfer from donor to acceptor [44]. The confirmation of the first pathway was achieved through the overlapping between the NCD’s excitation wavelength and dichloran absorption in the 275–400 nm range (red area), resulting in reduced excitation of the NCDs. The second pathway was established through the intersection of NCD emission and dichloran absorption in the 340–450 nm range (blue area), resulting in diminished fluorescence emission from the NCDs (Fig. 5b and c). These findings indicate that the IFE occurred as a quenching mechanism. Furthermore, the band gap (Eg) of NCDs and dichloran was estimated from the Tauc plot to investigate the quenching process (Fig. S1, supplementary materials). The Eg values for NCDs and dichloran were found to be 4.02 and 3.05 eV, respectively (Fig. 5c). The close resemblance and slightly higher Eg of NCDs compared to that of dichloran suggest the likelihood of competitive energy absorption and the emission energy transfer from NCDs was sufficient to excite dichloran. Consequently, these findings lead us to conclude that the favorable IFE resulted in a notable decrease in the fluorescence emission of NCDs in the presence of dichloran. Moreover, the NCDs revealed the presence of CC bonds in localized sp2 clusters and confirmed the existence of CO bonds. This was evident from the pronounced UV absorption bands at 285 and 325 nm, along with the identification of CC/CO bonds supported by the XPS and FT-IR data. Thus, it is possible that the effective interaction of the IFE is facilitated by close proximity resulting from the strong π-π interaction between the NCD’s large aromatic core and aromatic unit of dichloran, as well as the interaction between their polar functional groups.

Changes in pH, mixing time, and temperature can influence the sensor system. Hence, an investigation of the fluorescence emission intensities of NCDs in the presence of dichloran was conducted under various conditions. The effects of pH on the F/F0 ratios were then measured (Fig. 5d). The maximum fluorescence quenching was shown at pH 7, according to the lowest F/F0 value. Therefore, neutral pH was chosen for dichloran determination. Next, the response of fluorescence quenching to mixing time between NCDs and dichloran was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 5e. The F/F0 values remained remarkably consistent over a range from 30 sec to 10 min. The rapid quenching in fluorescence could be attributed to an effective IFE and strong interactions between NCDs and dichloran. These findings highlight the ability of the sensor system to deliver rapid and reliable performance within a short timeframe. The long-term stability of the NCDs was further investigated through periodic measurement of their fluorescence emission at various time intervals. It was found that the relative fluorescence intensities at 400 nm consistently stayed above 85 % after 2 months (Fig. S2). This substantiates the robustness and high stability of the NCDs.

In order to interpret the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs by dichloran, the contributions of both static and dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanisms were evaluated. Moreover, the temperature response of the fluorescence quenching of NCDs by dichloran was investigated. The F/F0 ratios were found to decrease linearly with increasing temperature, ranging from 10 to 80 °C (Fig. 5f). This indicates that fluorescence quenching increased with rising temperature, suggesting that dynamic fluorescence quenching occurred due to an increase in collisional quenching in the excited state [45]. Additionally, dynamic fluorescence quenching was investigated through the decay lifetime analysis of NCDs in the absence and presence of various concentrations of dichloran, where a slight change in the fluorescence lifetime decay curve was observed (Fig. S3a). This corresponded to the average fluorescence lifetime decay (τav ) of NCDs, showing significant variation with values of 1.66, 1.61, 1.55, and 1.36 ns when NCDs were exposed to a dichloran concentration of 0, 200, 500, and 1000 µM, respectively (Fig. S3b). In particular, the τav profiles of NCDs showed a linear declining trend across a concentration range from 0 to 1,000 µM (Fig. S3c). A lifetime decay quenching of approximately 18 % at 1,000 µM of dichloran indicates that dynamic quenching predominantly occurred [46]. Several previous studies have reported similar behavior. For example, Das, N. K. et al., reported the lifetime decay quenching of approximately 15 % at the same quencher concentration in their study on BSA sensing induced by DTAB [46]. Furthermore, the variation in the fluorescence decay lifetime ratio (τ 0/τ) versus dichloran concentration, where τ 0 and τ represent the fluorescence decay lifetimes of NCDs in the absence and presence of dichloran, respectively, was analyzed. The slope of the τ0/τ ratio showed a definite linear increase with rising dichloran concentration (Fig. S3d), thereby leading to the rational conclusion that the quenching occurred due to a collisional quenching mechanism, resulting in a positive deviation from linearity [47]. To further validate the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs showed no significant changes in the existing peaks and no new peaks appeared with increasing dichloran concentrations (Fig. S4) [48]. This indicates that static quenching was not as significant as the dynamic quenching. As previously noted, increasing dichloran concentration alters fluorescence decay lifetimes, indicating dynamic quenching between NCDs and dichloran [27,48].

According to the quenching mechanism for IFE described above, and to confirm the role of IFE-induced quenching in suppressing NCD fluorescence, IFE corrections were applied following Lakowicz's previously established method, as illustrated in Equation 3 [49,50].
(3)

A

+
A

) / 2
where Fcorr and Fobs denote the fluorescence intensity of NCDs after (corrected) and before (observed) the IFE correction, respectively, while Aex and Aem represent the UV–vis absorbance values at the excitation and emission wavelengths of NCDs, respectively. This correction was applied under varying dichloran concentrations introduced into the NCD solution.

As a result, the emission curve of Fcorr and Fobs exhibited a descending trend as the concentration of dichloran increased (Fig. S5a), highlighting significantly different variations in fluorescence intensity between the corrected and observed values [50]. This is due to the ability of the dichloran to absorb the emitted light from NCDs, based on the overlap between its absorbance and the emission and excitation wavelengths of NCDs, thereby contributing to the increased non-fluorescence of NCDs [51]. Moreover, the non-linear behavior observed in the Fobs,0 /Fobs and Fcorr,0 /Fcorr relationship (Fig. S5b) suggests that the IFE played an active role in influencing the sensing mechanism [51,52]. The parameters for the IFE corrections, including the correction factor (CF), the Fcorr /Fobs ratio, as well as the observed (Eobs ) and corrected (Ecorr ) fluorescence quenching efficiencies at various dichloran concentrations, were calculated in Table S1. Consequently, the CF values exhibit a progressive increase with the elevated concentrations of dichloran (Fig. S5c) [52]. Additionally, the findings further demonstrated that the quenching efficiency (E%) attributed to IFE for NCDs exhibited a proportional increase with rising dichloran concentrations from 0 to 200 µM, reaching approximately 95 % (Fig. S5d), suggesting that the fluorescence suppression of NCDs was predominantly caused by the IFE mechanism [53]. Nonetheless, the fluorescence quenching in NCD-based dichloran detection predominantly involves both IFE and dynamic quenching mechanisms, which affected and reduced the fluorescence intensity. These mechanisms can occur alongside but operate through distinct processes. Dynamic quenching arises from collisional interactions, affecting fluorescence decay lifetimes and revealing changes in the excited-state duration. Conversely, IFE, caused by spectral overlap between NCDs and dichloran, led to significant suppression of fluorescence intensity after IFE correction, without altering the fluorescence lifetime [54]. It is noteworthy to point out that the IFE-induced quenching efficiency increased with higher dichloran concentrations, while the dynamic quenching efficiency percentage decreased proportionally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of dichloran and paper-based platform
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantitative detection of dichloran was performed using the NCDs as the sensing probes. Various dichloran concentrations (0.00, 1.00, 3.00, 5.00, 10.0, 15.00, 20.00, 50.00, 75.00, 100.00, and 200.00 µM) were introduced into the NCDs solution at pH 7. The fluorescence emission of NCDs reduced significantly, accompanied by a slight red shift in the peak wavelength as the dichloran concentrations were increased. This suggests that dichloran affected the radiative surface states of the NCDs via the IFE interactions between the two materials. Approximately 74 and 91 % of the fluorescence intensity were quenched with the dichloran concentrations of 100 and 200 µM, respectively (Fig. 6 a). While the red-shifted emission can be attributed to 1) the influence of the surrounding hydrophilic solvent [55] and 2) alterations in the HOMO-LUMO gap of the NCDs, a relatively gradual lowering of the energy band gap as the dichloran concentration increased caused a shift toward longer wavelengths [56]. The Stern-Volmer relationship between F0/F values and dichloran concentrations was fitted as depicted in Fig. 6b. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained using 3
σ / s l o p e
where
σ
is the standard deviation of the blank fluorescence signal without adding dichloran (n = 5). The fitting equation was found to be F0/F = 0.0207x + 0.9826 (R2 = 0.9968), ranging from 1.0 to 50.0 µM, yielding an LOD of 7.6 nM (inset of Fig. 6b). Furthermore, our proposed method was compared to the other sensing platforms for the determination of dichloran, as displayed in Table 1 . It can be concluded that the sensing performance of our NCD sensing probes was among the best, attributed to their low LOD and wide linear range detection. Furthermore, our NCD materials were easily prepared and environmentally friendly, surpassing alternatives in sustainability. To our knowledge, this work represents the pioneering use of NCDs as sensing materials in a fluorescence sensor designed for dichloran detection. The paper-based platforms fabricated by NCDs loaded on filter paper in the presence of different dichloran concentrations (0.00, 5.00, 10.00, 25.00, 50.00, 75.00, 100.00, 150.00, 200.00, 300.00, 400.00, and 500.00 µM) were also examined (Fig. 6c). The digital images reveal a shift in the blue fluorescence emission towards a darker shade as the concentration of dichloran increased under a 365 nm UV lamp, confirming that the rising dichloran concentrations led to further fluorescence reduction. Subsequently, the sum of RGB values calculated from Eq. 1 decreased with increasing dichloran concentrations (Fig. 6d). The Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 6d illustrates RGB0/RGB values versus dichloran concentrations, where RGB0 represents the sum of RGB values obtained from the fluorescence emission of the bare NCD-paper. The LOD was found to be 4.24 µM based on the fitting equation: RGB0/RGB = 0.0021x + 1.0797 (R2 = 0.9983), ranging from 10 to 150 µM. The higher LOD of the paper-based sensors than that of the solution-based ones can be explained by the following reasons: 1) the heterogeneity of the sample on the carbon dot-coated paper leading to a high σ value and 2) the lesser sensitivity of the RGB scale and photo-capturing device leading to a small slope. However, this study demonstrated that the NCD-paper-based sensor offers a straightforward platform for portable on-site testing devices. It also exhibited high sensitivity in detecting dichloran, attributed to the strong interaction between the highly polar NCDs and cellulose in the filter paper, as well as the interaction between NCDs and dichloran.

The selectivity of the proposed fluorescence method towards dichloran was examined using the NCDs sensing probes. Various potential interfering substances, including metal ions, organic chemicals, and fungicides, such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl-,
N O
3
-
,
S O
4
2 -
, ascorbic acid, citric acid, tebuconazole, and vinclozolin, commonly present in agricultural crops, were considered. Tebuconazole and vinclozolin were selected for the interference analysis because these fungicides can be found in vegetables and fruits. In addition, their structures contain aromatic units similar to those of dichloran. The fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs/dichloran in the existence of interferences were measured. As the control sample, the fluorescence emission of NCDs was quenched with the addition of dichloran. However, the fluorescence emission of NCDs/dichloran with interferences closely resembled that of the control sample without interferences, with relative fluorescence intensities in a range of 93.0–101.8 % (Fig. 8 a and b). This indicated that the interfering substances did not significantly affect the NCDs/dichloran system. Furthermore, the selectivity of NCD-paper towards dichloran based on the paper-based sensor was performed (Fig. 8c). The filter paper coated with bare NCDs exhibited a blue fluorescence emission, whereas the fluorescence emission of NCD-paper in the presence of dichloran was significantly quenched, resulting in a dark blue color. Notably, the blue fluorescence emission of NCD-paper in the presence of interferences (except for dichloran) remained similar to that of the bare NCD-paper. These findings were consistent with the interference analysis using fluorescence spectroscopy. In summary, the proposed fluorescence sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity towards dichloran, even in the presence of various metal ions, chemicals, and other fungicides, in the solution and on the paper-based sensors.

The novel fluorescence sensor employing label-free nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) presents a promising platform for the sensitive, selective, and fast detection of the dichloran fungicide. The self-passivated fluorescent NCDs were successfully prepared through a one-step pyrolysis process using maleic anhydride and diethylenetriamine. This detection was achieved by quenching NCD’s fluorescence in the presence of dichloran, a phenomenon attributed to the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching, forming the basis of the sensor’s mechanism. The selectivity of the NCDs towards dichloran was evident in the presence of interferences, such as common metal ions, chemicals, and other fungicides. Detecting dichloran in carrots, grapes, and drinking water consistently delivered satisfactory and accurate results. The paper-based sensor also demonstrated high efficacy in selectively detecting dichloran amidst interferences, adding versatility to its application. Through the development of sensors both in solution- and paper-based platforms, field-employable detection or onsite testing is possible and will offer significant impacts here. However, the limitation of the carbon dots lies in the low product yield, which can be alleviated through the use of other precursors with higher carbon content. Furthermore, the LOD value of the paper-based sensor is still in the micromolar range, which should be improved. Therefore, future work may be performed to improve the carbon dot yield and the sensitivity of the paper-based sensor through systematic optimization of precursors/reaction conditions and sensing platforms, respectively. Notably, this study contributes significantly to the advancement of efficient and portable detection platforms, particularly in the realms of environmental monitoring and agriculture, by introducing a robust fluorescence sensor for the detection of dichloran, a harmful fungicide.
